model.label: Gemini 3 Flash
model.description: Google DeepMind • Fast Gemini model optimized for low-latency reasoning, multimodal inputs, and tool use.

pick.rationale: This Final Four matchup features two of the nation's most elite and balanced teams, both ranking in the top 10 for offensive efficiency and the top 5 for defensive efficiency. While Michigan boasts a slightly higher overall analytical profile and the nation's 2nd-ranked defense, they are navigating the tournament with a thinned rotation following season-ending injuries to key backups LJ Cason and Cole Sullivan. Arizona enters at full strength with star freshman Koa Peat and multiple projected NBA talents, providing a higher individual ceiling and superior depth in a high-stakes setting. Following a battle-tested run that included a victory over a top-tier Purdue squad, the Wildcats' elite defensive length and transition game should allow them to narrowly outpace the Wolverines and advance to the title game.

reasoningStep.title: National Semifinal: Arizona vs. Michigan
reasoningStep.summary: A clash of titans between two 1-seeds with top-3 analytical profiles. Arizona's superior depth and high-end talent give them a slight edge over a shorthanded Michigan squad.
evidence: Michigan ranks #2 and Arizona #3 in overall efficiency according to KenPom and Torvik.
Michigan is missing key rotation players LJ Cason and Cole Sullivan due to season-ending injuries.
Arizona's star freshman Koa Peat is healthy and leads a roster with multiple projected NBA talents.
Arizona's path to the Final Four included a victory over #2 seed Purdue, while Michigan's highest-seeded win was over #4 seed Texas Tech.

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

result.text: Meet the 2025-26 Michigan Wolverines men's basketball team Skip to main content Home Sports Lions Tigers Red Wings Pistons Michigan Michigan State Big Ten More Colleges High School Soccer Scoreboards Meet the 2025-26 Michigan men's basketball team: A player-by-player breakdown James Hawkins The Detroit News James Hawkins of The Detroit News provides a player-by-player breakdown of the 2025-26 Michigan men’s basketball roster. Backcourt Nimari Burnett ▶ Year: Graduate student ▶ Height: 6-5 ▶ Weight: 195 ▶ Analysis: Burnett has started all 69 games he’s played at Michigan the past two seasons and will remain a fixture in the starting lineup. He can take on ball-handling duties if needed, but he doesn’t need to have the ball in his hands for long to be effective, especially as a catch-and-shoot 3-point threat. On a team where 3-point shooting is a question mark, there’s no question that’s his greatest asset to this team — he made a team-leading 66 3-pointers at a career-high 40% clip last season — along with his leadership and steadiness. Playing in the same system and for the same coach for the first time in his college career should also benefit all parties. “His level of consistency, his routine, his mentorship to the young guys, he brings so much to the table,” head coach Dusty May said. Elliot Cadeau ▶ Year: Junior ▶ Height: 6-1 ▶ Weight: 180 ▶ Analysis: The North Carolina transfer will run the show after serving as the Tar Heels’ starting point guard the past two seasons. Cadeau has elite speed and is dynamic in the open court, which should lead to more transition offense. Turnovers were a problem for both Cadeau and the Wolverines last season, and cutting down those numbers will be paramount. But the key to making everything work might be Cadeau’s perimeter shooting — he’s shot 28.1% from deep in 74 career games — and whether opposing defenses respect him enough as a shooter in pick-and-rolls. “He takes pride in making other guys better. That's a rare quality, I would say, in today's day and age,” assistant coach Justin Joyner said. “He's one of those guys that loves to see guys get love, make shots, get dunks. He feeds off that. And then his competitive energy on the defensive end, especially when he's engaged on the ball, when he switches on the bigs … he's got a great fight to him.” L.J. Cason ▶ Year: Sophomore ▶ Height: 6-2 ▶ Weight: 195 ▶ Analysis: Cason will look to build off last season’s March moments as he takes on a bigger role, particularly as a ball-handler when Cadeau isn’t on the floor. His ability to get into the lane will be needed, as will his ability to flip the switch between facilitator and scorer. He’s more confident and more mature after navigating some bumpy stretches last season and is taking a more professional approach into Year 2 , which has him ripe to make a sophomore leap. “His passing and playmaking are what's going to stand out to the fans,” assistant coach and general manager Kyle Church said. “We knew he coul

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
